letters.Author: Philip Garbo
letters.LetterText:
To the Editor, The Press
Rabbi Brickner recently made some remarks on the radio about which I wish to comment and in some instances take exception to. He said that Hitler has become chancellor because Von Hindenburg is old and his days are numbered. Does the rabbi mean that 60,000,000 people in Germany are asleep that they allow this man to take advantage of them? Certainly Von Hindenburg cannot have lost control of his mind.
In one breath he expresses concern about the March 5 elections and that Hitler will drive the Jews from Germany and in the next, the rabbi likens Hitler to Mussolini. How can he make such a comparison when it is common knowledge that in the years Mussolini has been in power, the Jews in Italy have received better treatment and more consideration than in any other country in the world. Should such a thing happen in Germany as the driving out of the Jews, an appeal can be made to Mussolini and the Pope and they will welcome the Jews to Italy.
He said that Germany, Italy, Austria and Hungary are banding together against France, Jugoslavia and Russia (with Poland on the fence) and that there will soon be war. This is just a bad dream. Mussolini has been in power for more than 10 years and in spite of France's innuendos he has kept and is keeping peace. He has been on the fields of battle, wounded several times and he knows what war means.
Of course, it is evident that the rabbi is against Mussolini because he is a dictator. He does not tell his listeners of the good that Mussolini has done for Italy, bringing it to its rightful place as a sovereign nation. Think how much better off we would be were Roosevelt vested with the powers of a dictator. Then the kind of corruption in government we read about in Liberty magazine would be rectified.
letters.Summary: The letter responds to Rabbi Brickner's radio comments on Hitler and Mussolini. Philip Garbo criticizes the rabbi for comparing Hitler to Mussolini, pointing out that Jews in Italy have received better treatment under Mussolini. He also disagrees with the rabbi's statement that Germany, Italy, Austria, and Hungary are banding together against France, Jugoslavia, and Russia. Garbo suggests that if Roosevelt had dictatorial powers, he could address corruption in government.
letters.Title: Differs With Rabbi Brickner

letters.Author: C. B. Keenum
letters.LetterText:
To the Editor, The Press
This bank limitation of 5 per cent on withdrawals that has just reached Cleveland reminds one of the recent statement made by Secretary Mills that the total bank deposits in the U. S. A. amounts to $46,000,000,000 and the actual cash in the U. S. A. amounts to less than $6,000,000,000. If our Congress would now print $40,000,000,000 in currency there would then be just enough money in the country to cover the bank deposits and if our government should print $40,000,000,000 on its own instead of the usual 'bank notes' and loan the same to refinance farm and home loans at 2 per cent interest, the revenue to the government would almost balance the budget and, at the same time force down all interest rates to 2 per cent and thus save a difference in interest burden on the American people of 4 per cent average on the 200,000,000,000 total of interest bearing debts, or $8,000,-000,000.
This $8,000,000,000 a year saving to the people would automatically increase the buying power of the masses to that extent. It would do more to 'restore confidence' and create prosperity than all the public works programs and beer tax programs and foreign trade programs all put together could do. Let's put Cleveland to work but not by inflating our debt structure with more bond issues. Our banker friends are hollering their heads off against an inflation of currency, but you don't hear one of them saying a word about us having inflated our debts so much that just the interest now amounts to $4,000,000 a year more than our entire money supply.
For us to try and run this country with less than $6,000,000,000 (plus bank credit confidence) is like trying to run a motor car with one pint of oil in the crank case.
letters.Summary: The letter argues that printing $40 billion in currency would solve the bank deposit crisis and balance the budget. It also suggests that refinancing farm and home loans at 2 percent interest would force down all interest rates to 2 percent, saving the American people $8 billion per year.
letters.Title: Wants Currency Relief

letters.Author: Philip Garbo
letters.LetterText:
To the Editor, The Press
Rabbi Brickner recently made some remarks on the radio about which I wish to comment and in some instances take exception to. He said that Hitler has become chancellor because Von Hindenburg is old and his days are numbered. Does the rabbi mean that 60,000,000 people in Germany are asleep that they allow this man to take advantage of them? Certainly Von Hindenburg cannot have lost control of his mind.
In one breath he expresses concern about the March 5 elections and that Hitler will drive the Jews from Germany and in the next, the rabbi likens Hitler to Mussolini. How can he make such a comparison when it is common knowledge that in the years Mussolini has been in power, the Jews in Italy have received better treatment and more consideration than in any other country in the world. Should such a thing happen in Germany as the driving out of the Jews, an appeal can be made to Mussolini and the Pope and they will welcome the Jews to Italy.
He said that Germany, Italy, Austria and Hungary are banding together against France, Jugoslavia and Russia (with Poland on the fence) and that there will soon be war. This is just a bad dream. Mussolini has been in power for more than 10 years and in spite of France's innuendos he has kept and is keeping peace. Of course, it is evident that the rabbi is against Mussolini because he is a dictator. He does not tell his listeners of the good that Mussolini has done for Italy, bringing it to its rightful place as a sovereign nation. Think how much better off we would be were Roosevelt vested with the powers of a dictator. Then the kind of corruption in government we read about in Liberty magazine would be rectified.

Philip Garbo criticizes the rabbi for comparing Hitler to Mussolini, pointing out that Jews in Italy have received better treatment under Mussolini. He also disagrees with the rabbi's statement that Germany, Italy, Austria, and Hungary are banding together against France, Jugoslavia, and Russia. Garbo suggests that if Roosevelt had dictatorial powers, he could address corruption in government.
